word_list.pronunciation: ɐ hˈaɪv bˈiːhaɪv ʌv æktˈɪvɪɾi ˈɪndʌstɹi

senses.definition: a place where many people are very busy

examples.x: The airport terminal was a beehive of activity as travelers hurried to catch their flights.
The factory floor was a hive of industry, with workers rushing to meet their production targets.
The newsroom was a hive of activity as reporters rushed to get their stories out before the deadline.
The park was a beehive of industry on the weekend, with families playing, dogs running around, and people having picnics.

word_list.pronunciation: biː ɡɛt ɹˈʌn ɹˈʌʃt ˈɔf wˈʌnz fˈiːt

senses.definition: to have a lot of things that need to be done

examples.x: If we don't hire more staff, we're going to get rushed off our feet during the busy season.
The catering team was rushed off their feet during the wedding reception, making sure all the guests were served.
The store was rushed off its feet on Black Friday, with customers streaming in all day.
The nurses were rushed off their feet dealing with emergency cases all night.

word_list.pronunciation: hæv ɐ lˈɑːt ɔːɹ tˈuː mʌtʃ ˌɑːn wˈʌnz plˈeɪt

senses.definition: to have a certain amount of tasks or problems that need to be dealt with

examples.x: I can't take on any more work right now--I already have a lot on my plate.
If you're feeling overwhelmed, it's probably because you have too much on your plate right now.
The CEO was juggling multiple projects, each one a major undertaking, so she had a lot on her plate.
With the upcoming election, the politician had a lot on his plate, including campaign events, fundraising, and strategy meetings.
He didn’t accept the invitation since he already had too much on his plate.

senses.definition: to work really hard to achieve an aim

examples.x: After running five miles in the scorching heat, I was sweating my guts out.
The hikers slogged their guts out, trekking through the muddy terrain for hours to reach the summit.

senses.definition: extremely busy with work, activities, etc.

examples.x: Ever since she started her own business, she's been as busy as a bee every day.
I have a lot of work to do, so I'm going to be busy as a bee all day today.

word_list.pronunciation: wˈɜːk lˈaɪk ɐ hˈɔːɹs dˈɑːɡ tɹˈoʊdʒən

examples.x: The farmer worked like a horse during the harvest season, putting in long hours to bring in the crops.

word_list.pronunciation: bˈɜːn ðə kˈændəl æt bˈoʊθ ˈɛndz

senses.definition: to drain oneself by working very late into the night and getting up early in the morning

examples.x: I used to burn the candles at both ends when I was in college, but I've learned to pace myself since then.
She's been burning the candle at both ends lately, working long hours at her job and staying up late to study for her exams.
He's always busy as a beaver building a new dam, working on his art and his music and his writing.
She's been busy as a beaver lately, juggling her job, her family, and her volunteer work.

senses.definition: to have a lot of work that needs to be dealt with

examples.x: If you're thinking of starting your own business, be prepared to have your hands full with all the work that needs to be done.
She has her hands full with her three kids and her job, so she doesn't have a lot of free time.
The project manager has his hands full trying to coordinate the team and meet the project deadline.
I can’t take on any more tasks—I already have my hands full.
After the promotion, she had her hands full with more duties than ever before.

senses.definition: in a state that is extremely efficient or active

examples.x: She's been in high gear all week, finishing her projects ahead of schedule and taking on new tasks.
The athletes were in high gear during the final stretch of the race, pushing themselves to the limit to cross the finish line.
The company has been in high gear since the launch of their new product, with sales and production at an all-time high.
With the deadline approaching, the team kicked into high gear and worked around the clock to finish the project.

word_list.pronunciation: nˌɑːt hæv ɐ mˈɪnɪt tə kˈɔːl wˈʌnz ˈoʊn

senses.definition: to be so busy that one has no free time

examples.x: If we want to meet the deadline, we're all going to have to work overtime and not have a minute to call our own.
The students were so busy with their coursework and extracurricular activities that they didn't have a minute to call their own during the school year
With all the demands of running a small business, he doesn't have a minute to call his own these days.

senses.definition: in a state of being actively engaged in various activities or constantly in motion, typically indicating a busy and active lifestyle

examples.x: If you want to keep up with her, you've got to be on the go too.
She's always on the go, juggling her job, her family, and her social life.
The mobile app allows you to manage tasks on the go.
Even while traveling, he remains on the go with business calls.
She’s always on the go with work and family duties.

senses.definition: to try to handle or manage several difficult tasks or responsibilities at the same time, highlighting a busy and demanding situation

examples.x: I've got so many things to do at the moment, I feel like I'm juggling frogs.
One hundred years from now, will we remember the sitcom star, or the surgeon, or the human rights activist, or the guy who juggled frogs?
We get the first glimpse of his brilliance when he juggles frogs during his adolescence.

senses.definition: to take on more tasks or responsibilities than one can handle effectively, resulting in a lack of focus and decreased productivity

examples.x: If we try to do everything at once, we'll spread ourselves too thin and won't be able to do anything well.
She's been spreading herself too thin lately, taking on too many projects at work and not having enough time to rest or spend time with her family.
The manager warned us not to spread ourselves too thin by taking on too many clients at once.
If we don't start working on this project soon, we'll be pressed for time and won't be able to finish it before the deadline.
perfect tense: She has been pressed for time lately because she's been working on a big project at work.
Yesterday, I was really pressed for time, so I had to skip lunch to finish my work on time.

senses.definition: used to indicate that a situation is becoming highly intense or critical, often implying increased pressure, urgency, or scrutiny
